title: Week 8
text:
This week focuses on recommendation systems, including:

Association rules (apriori)
Customer segmentation
Collaborative filtering
Ethical considerations
Hyperparameters and optimisation
Graph Analytics

title: Week 8
text:
Recommendation Systems
A recommendation system suggests items to users based on data.

Types:

Association rule-based (Apriori)
Content-based filtering
Collaborative filtering

title: Week 8
text:
Association Rules (Apriori)
Used to find relationships between items.

Example:
Customers who buy bread also buy butter


Key measures:

Support → how often items appear together

Confidence → probability of buying Y given X

Lift → strength of the relationship

👉 In practice: - focus on high lift rules - filter out weak or obvious relationships

title: Week 8
text:
Customer Segmentation
Before applying rules, customers are often grouped.

Why?
Different groups behave differently.


Example:

Students → low budget
Professionals → higher spending

👉 Apply recommendation rules within each segment

title: Week 8
text:
Collaborative Filtering
This is a key concept for exams.

Definition
Collaborative filtering makes recommendations based on similar users.

title: Week 8
text:
How it works

Collect user preferences (ratings, purchases, clicks)
Find users with similar behaviour
Recommend items liked by similar users

title: Week 8
text:
Example

User A and User B both like:

Netflix shows A, B, C

User B also likes:

Show D


👉 Recommend Show D to User A

title: Week 8
text:
Types of Collaborative Filtering

1. User-based

Find similar users
Recommend based on their preferences



2. Item-based

Find similar items
Recommend similar items to what user already likes

title: Week 8
text:
Advantages

Simple and intuitive

Works well with large datasets

No need for item features

title: Week 8
text:
Limitations

Cold start problem (new users/items)

Requires lots of data

Can reinforce bias

title: Week 8
text:
Ethics in Recommendation Systems
Important exam topic ⚠️

Issues:

Privacy concerns

Bias and discrimination

Filter bubbles (only showing similar content)

Manipulation of user behaviour

title: Week 8
text:
Hyperparameters
Settings that control how a model learns.

Examples:

Number of clusters (k in k-means)
Number of neighbours in collaborative filtering

title: Week 8
text:
Hyperparameter Optimisation
Choosing the best values for hyperparameters.

Methods:

Trial and error

Grid search

Cross-validation

title: Week 8
text:
Graph Analytics
Graph analytics (or network analysis) is the study of networks or graphs. A network / graph has nodes and edges between them. The nodes and the edges may both have attributes.

Can be used in:

Marketing attribution analysis: this analyses the adverts shown to a user and the activities. It may show that indirect activities that are in an earlier step can have casual properties.
Customer purchase modeling: By modeling the sequence of purchases as a graph, you can generate recommendations of what customers are likely to buy next.
Fraud network detection - internet search engines: graph analytics are a core part of internet search algorithms. Relationship that exists in the transaction.
Disease cluster analysis: can be used to model and the spread of diseases

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 1 Notes
text:
2.1 Artificial Intelligence (AI)
AI is the broadest term. It refers to when computers can perform tasks that are human-like — tasks that normally require human intelligence. This covers a huge range of areas beyond just machine learning, including search algorithms, process mining, robotics, natural language understanding, and more.
A useful reference: the classic textbook by Peter Norvig on AI covers around 20 chapters, and only one or two are about machine learning — the rest cover other AI subfields.
Example: Google Maps performing route optimisation is a form of AI — it’s doing something that would normally require human intelligence to figure out.

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 1 Notes
text:
2.2 Machine Learning (ML)
Machine learning is a subset of AI. It uses statistical algorithms to allow machines to get better at tasks with more experience and data.
The key distinction from traditional software is that ML systems are stochastic (probabilistic), not deterministic:

Deterministic system (traditional software): The same input always produces the same output. Rules are hard-coded.
Stochastic system (ML): The output can change as the model learns from new data. A fraud detection model might classify a transaction as legitimate today, but after being retrained with new fraudulent examples, it might flag that same transaction pattern as fraudulent tomorrow.

This is the core idea: ML algorithms learn and adapt from data, rather than following fixed rules.

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 1 Notes
text:
2.3 Data Mining
Data mining means extracting rules and patterns from large quantities of data. It is an older term, rarely used in industry today. For practical purposes, consider it interchangeable with machine learning. This course uses the term in its title, but the content is fundamentally about machine learning.

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 1 Notes
text:
2.4 Deep Learning
Deep learning is a family of algorithms within machine learning that is especially good at:

Computer vision (image recognition, object detection)
Text analysis (natural language processing, language generation)

Deep learning is the foundation for Large Language Models (LLMs) like ChatGPT and for generative AI.

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 1 Notes
text:
2.5 How They Fit Together
Think of these as nested circles: AI is the largest circle, ML sits inside AI, and Deep Learning sits inside ML.
Concrete example — Self-driving car:

The self-driving car as a whole is AI (performing a human-like task)
It uses a Deep Learning model to “see” and interpret its surroundings
That Deep Learning model is a type of Machine Learning model, since Deep Learning is a subset of ML

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 1 Notes
text:
3.1 Marketing

Customer survey analysis using Natural Language Processing (NLP) to categorise feedback
Churn prediction: Identifying customers likely to leave before they decide to leave (typically 1–2 months ahead), so you can intervene. By the time someone decides to cancel, it’s usually too late.
Cross-selling and upselling: After someone buys a TV, they’re unlikely to buy another TV — but they might buy a soundbar or cables. Smart recommendation engines exploit this.
Customer segmentation: The famous Tesco Clubcard story — Tesco used clustering/segmentation on loyalty card data to group customers (e.g. “double income, no kids”, “young families”, “early retirees”) and then targeted marketing to each group. This was so valuable that Tesco bought the analytics company (Dunnhumby) to prevent competitors from accessing the same capability.

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 1 Notes
text:
3.2 Finance & Banking

Fraud detection: One of ML’s earliest and most critical successes. The entire contactless payment system relies on keeping fraud rates extremely low (well under 1%). If fraud rates climbed, people would lose trust and return to cash.
Credit risk: Automating loan decisions — but with important legal and ethical constraints (you cannot use ML as an excuse to discriminate based on race, gender, etc.)
Automated trading: Over 99% of stock exchange transactions are done by bots. Speed is critical — algorithms must be written in low-level languages (C++) and machines must be physically co-located near the exchange.
Document processing: Using AI/GenAI to extract and verify information from mortgage applications (bank statements, payslips, etc.)
Digital banks (Revolut, N26, Monzo) rely heavily on AI for operations since they have no physical branches.

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 1 Notes
text:
3.3 Manufacturing

Optimal production scheduling
Predicting supply and demand
Anomaly detection in production processes
Predictive maintenance — predicting when equipment will fail before it does

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 1 Notes
text:
3.4 Telecommunications

Speech recognition for customer service
Churn prediction (similar to marketing)
Analysing ad spend and promotions
Spatial data analysis for cell tower placement

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 1 Notes
text:
3.5 Pharmaceuticals

Manufacturing site data analysis
Drug discovery
Clinical trial research
Radiology and radiotherapy
Smart electronic health records
Epidemic outbreak prediction

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 1 Notes
text:
4.1 What is CRISP-DM?
CRISP-DM stands for CRoss Industry Standard Process for Data Mining. It is the most widely used process model for data science projects. It is an agile, iterative approach — the phases are not rigid, and you frequently move back and forth between them.

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 1 Notes
text:
4.2 The Six Phases

Business Understanding — What does the business actually need? What problem are we solving? This is where you define the objective.
Data Understanding — What data do we have? What data do we need? Is it clean? Is it complete? This is where you explore and get familiar with the data.
Data Preparation — How do we organise and transform the data for modelling? This includes cleaning, feature engineering, handling missing values, encoding variables, etc. This phase often takes the most time.
Modelling — What modelling techniques should we apply? This is where you train your ML algorithms on the prepared data.
Evaluation — Which model best meets the business objectives? Not just which has the best accuracy, but which actually solves the business problem.
Deployment — How do stakeholders access the results? How is the model put into production?

Critical point: The outcome of each phase determines what comes next. You might build a model, realise the data needs more preparation, go back to step 3, then return to modelling.

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 1 Notes
text:
4.3 Ethics and Fairness in ML
This is not optional — it is a core responsibility of anyone working in data science.
Key principle: ML is fundamentally a complex pattern matcher. If your data contains biases, the model will learn and reproduce those biases. The bias is almost always caused by the data reflecting existing real-world biases.
Real-world examples of ML bias:

Amazon’s recruiting tool was found to discriminate against women because it was trained on historical hiring data that was predominantly male.
Facial recognition systems have been shown to perform poorly on certain demographic groups.
Criminal justice algorithms have been criticised for producing biased risk assessments.

Your responsibility as a data scientist: You must be willing to stand up and say “I don’t think this model will work fairly” if you have concerns. The reputational damage companies suffer from deploying discriminatory ML models is severe.

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 1 Notes
text:
5.1 Supervised Learning
What it is: You have labelled data — both the input features (X) and the known outcome (Y). The algorithm learns the relationship between X and Y so it can predict Y for new, unseen data.
Key terminology:

Predictor variables (X): The features/columns you use to make predictions (also called independent variables or features)
Predicted variable (Y): What you are trying to predict (also called the target, dependent variable, or label)
Model training / Model building: The process of creating the ML model from historical data
Prediction / Inference: Using the trained model on new data to estimate Y

Two sub-types of supervised learning:

5.1.1 Classification
Predicting a category — the output is one of a discrete set of values.

Binary classification: Two possible outcomes (e.g. fraud/not fraud, spam/not spam, survived/died)
Multi-class classification: More than two possible outcomes (e.g. classifying an image as cat/dog/bird)

Common classification algorithms: Logistic Regression, Decision Trees, Support Vector Machines (SVM), Naive Bayes, Random Forests, Gradient Boosted Trees, Deep Learning


5.1.2 Regression
Predicting a continuous number — the output is a numerical value.

Examples: predicting house prices, predicting temperature, predicting sales revenue

Common regression algorithms: Linear Regression, Generalised Linear Models, Decision Trees, Random Forests, Deep Learning

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 1 Notes
text:
5.2 Unsupervised Learning
What it is: You have data but no labels — there is no Y variable. The algorithm tries to find hidden structure or patterns in the data on its own.
Key techniques:

Clustering / Segmentation: Grouping similar data points together (e.g. customer segmentation — the Tesco example above)
Dimensionality Reduction: Reducing the number of features while preserving important information (e.g. PCA, t-SNE)

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 1 Notes
text:
5.3 Reinforcement Learning
What it is: An algorithm learns by trial and error, receiving rewards or penalties for its actions. It adapts its strategy over time.
Examples: Self-driving cars, game-playing AI (like AlphaGo), robotics

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 1 Notes
text:
6.1 What is Linear Regression?
Linear regression finds the line of best fit through a set of data points. If you remember y = mx + b from school (where m is the slope and b is the y-intercept), that’s exactly what linear regression computes — but it finds the best values of m and b given noisy, real-world data.

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 1 Notes
text:
6.2 Setting Up the Data

set.seed(42) # Ensures reproducibility — same "random" numbers every time
m <- 1.4      # True slope
b <- -1.2     # True intercept
x <- 0:9      # X values: 0, 1, 2, ..., 9

y <- m * x + b              # The TRUE relationship (green line)
py <- y + rnorm(10)          # Observed values = true + random noise

What’s happening here:

We define a “true” linear relationship: y = 1.4x - 1.2
y represents the perfect, noise-free values (the green line in the plot)
py adds random noise using rnorm(10) — this simulates real-world data, which is never perfectly on the line
set.seed(42) makes the random numbers reproducible — anyone running this code gets the same results

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 1 Notes
text:
6.3 Building and Plotting the Model

# Plot the true line (green) and observed data points (X marks)
plot(x, y, type = "l", col = "green",
     main = paste("y = ", m, "x ", b, sep = ""))
points(x, py, pch = 4)

# Fit a linear regression model
model1 <- lm(py ~ x)

# Add the predicted line (blue dashed)
lines(x, model1$coefficients[2] * x + model1$coefficients[1],
      lty = 2, col = "blue")

Understanding lm(py ~ x):

lm stands for “linear model” — it’s R’s function for linear regression
py ~ x is a formula: it means “model py as a function of x”
The function returns a model object with properties like coefficients

The plot shows three things:

Green line: The true underlying relationship (which in real life we wouldn’t know)
X marks: The observed/measured data points (noisy)
Blue dashed line: The model’s best estimate of the relationship

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 1 Notes
text:
6.4 Interpreting the Model Output

model1
## Coefficients:
## (Intercept)         x
##    -0.7895    1.4304

The model estimated:

Intercept = -0.7895 (true value was -1.2)
Slope = 1.4304 (true value was 1.4)

These are close but not exact, because the model is working from noisy data. This is a fundamental concept: models are approximations of reality.

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 1 Notes
text:
6.5 The Model Summary

summary(model1)

The summary() function gives detailed statistical information, including:

Coefficients table: Shows the estimate, standard error, t-value, and p-value for each coefficient
Significance codes: Stars (***) indicate statistically significant variables
R-squared: A measure of how well the model fits the data

Important lecture insight on coefficients: Even if a variable is statistically significant (has stars), you should also look at the magnitude of its coefficient. If the coefficient is tiny (e.g. 0.000001), multiplying it by the X values won’t meaningfully affect predictions — so it may not be practically important, even if it’s statistically significant. Always consider both the statistical significance AND the scale/magnitude.

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 1 Notes
text:
6.6 Understanding R-squared

r_squared1 <- cor(x, py) ^ 2
r_squared1
## [1] 0.9645404

What is R-squared?

It measures the goodness of fit of a regression model
It ranges from 0 to 1, where 1 means a perfect fit
It can be calculated as the square of the correlation between X and Y
An R-squared of 0.9645 means approximately 96.45% of the variance in the data is explained by the model

What is correlation?

Correlation measures the strength and direction of the linear relationship between two variables
It ranges from -1 to +1
+1: Perfect positive relationship (as one increases, the other increases proportionally)
-1: Perfect negative relationship (as one increases, the other decreases proportionally)
0: No linear relationship
Generally, correlations above 0.7 (or below -0.7) indicate a strong relationship

Critical practical advice from the lecturer: If you ever see an R-squared above ~0.85–0.90 on real-world tabular data, be suspicious. It might indicate data leakage (a concept covered in Week 5) or that the problem is so simple it doesn’t need ML. In reality, real-world datasets are messy and an R-squared this high is unusual.

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 1 Notes
text:
6.7 The Effect of More Noise
The lecture then re-runs the same analysis with more randomness added to the data:

py <- y + rnorm(10)  # New random noise (different from before since set.seed not called again)

model2 <- lm(py ~ x)
r_squared2 <- cor(x, py) ^ 2
r_squared2
## [1] 0.8524506

Key takeaway: With more noise (randomness) in the data:

The R-squared dropped from 0.9645 to 0.8525
The blue line (model prediction) diverges more from the green line (true relationship)
This demonstrates that noisier data leads to less accurate models — the model struggles more when the signal-to-noise ratio is lower

This is a fundamental concept you’ll see throughout the course: the quality and cleanliness of your data directly impacts how well any model can perform.

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 1 Notes
text:
6.8 The General Linear Regression Formula
For multiple predictor variables, the formula extends to:
\[\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \beta_3 x_3 + \dots + \beta_n x_n\]
Where:

\(\hat{y}\) is the predicted value
\(\beta_0\) is the intercept
\(\beta_1, \beta_2, \dots, \beta_n\) are the coefficients (learned from the data)
\(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\) are the input feature values

Each coefficient is multiplied by its corresponding feature value and summed together. The model learns the optimal coefficient values during training.

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 1 Notes
text:
6.9 Gradient Descent (Brief Introduction)
The lecturer briefly mentioned gradient descent — the foundational algorithm that many ML models use to find the best-fit parameters:

Start with an initial guess for the line of best fit
Shift it slightly in one direction
Check: did the fit improve?
If yes, keep going in that direction; if no, try the other direction
Repeat until you find the minimum error

This iterative optimisation process is the basis for training many ML algorithms, from linear regression to SVMs to deep learning.

title: Data Mining — Week 4 Notes
text:
This week covers several important topics in data mining and machine learning:

Evaluation metrics for classification (beyond accuracy): precision, recall, F1 score, ROC/AUC
Logistic regression as a classification algorithm
Precision/Recall trade-off and how to manipulate the decision threshold
Evaluation metrics for regression: MAE, MSE, RMSE, R²

title: Data Mining — Week 4 Notes
text:
2 Evaluation Metrics for Classification

2.1 Why Accuracy Alone Is Not Enough
Accuracy is the simplest metric: it measures the percentage of predictions that are correct.
\[\text{Accuracy} = \frac{\text{Correct Predictions}}{\text{Total Predictions}}\]
However, accuracy can be misleading in two important situations:

When the cost of errors is not equal across classes. In medical diagnosis, predicting a sick person as healthy (false negative) is far more dangerous than predicting a healthy person as sick (false positive). Accuracy treats both errors the same.
When the dataset is imbalanced. If only 1% of credit card transactions are fraudulent, a model that simply says “no fraud” for every transaction achieves 99% accuracy — but catches zero actual fraud. The accuracy looks great, but the model is useless for the task it was designed for.



2.2 The Confusion Matrix
A confusion matrix is a table that breaks down all predictions into four categories. For a binary problem where we call one class “Positive” (the thing we’re trying to detect — e.g., survived, sick, fraudulent) and the other “Negative”:




Actual Positive
Actual Negative




Predicted Positive
True Positive (TP)
False Positive (FP)


Predicted Negative
False Negative (FN)
True Negative (TN)




True Positive (TP): The model correctly predicted Positive. (e.g., patient is sick and model said sick)
True Negative (TN): The model correctly predicted Negative. (e.g., patient is healthy and model said healthy)
False Positive (FP): The model incorrectly predicted Positive. (e.g., patient is healthy but model said sick)
False Negative (FN): The model incorrectly predicted Negative. (e.g., patient is sick but model said healthy)

Titanic example from class: With Survived=1 as our positive class:
         Actual
Predicted  1   0
       1  18   4    ← Model predicted survived
       0  18  50    ← Model predicted did not survive
So: TP=18, FP=4, FN=18, TN=50.


2.3 Precision
Precision answers the question: “Of all the cases the model predicted as Positive, how many actually were Positive?”
\[\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}\]
In plain English (using disease example): “Of the people the model said were sick, what percentage actually were sick?”
Titanic example: Precision = 18 / (18 + 4) = 0.82 (82%)
High precision means that when the model says “Positive,” you can trust it — there are few false alarms.


2.4 Recall (Sensitivity)
Recall answers the question: “Of all the cases that actually were Positive, how many did the model find?”
\[\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}\]
In plain English: “Of the people who were actually sick, what percentage did the model identify?”
Titanic example: Recall = 18 / (18 + 18) = 0.50 (50%)
High recall means the model catches most of the actual positive cases — it doesn’t miss many.
Tip from the lecturer to remember the difference: Recall looks at the actual values (the column in the confusion matrix). Precision looks at the predicted values (the row in the confusion matrix).


2.5 F1 Score
The F1 score is the harmonic mean of precision and recall. It balances both metrics into a single number.
\[F1 = 2 \times \frac{\text{Precision} \times \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}\]
Why the harmonic mean instead of a simple average? Because the harmonic mean punishes extreme values. A model with Precision = 1.0 and Recall = 0.0 has a simple average of 0.5, but an F1 of 0.0. This makes F1 a fairer metric — it forces both precision and recall to be reasonably good.
Titanic example: F1 = 2 × (0.82 × 0.50) / (0.82 + 0.50) ≈ 0.62


2.6 Communicating Metrics to Non-Technical People
This was strongly emphasised in class: business people and domain experts will not understand F1, precision, and recall by name. You need to translate these into plain language:

Instead of “Recall is 0.50” → “Of those who are sick, the model finds 50% of the cases.”
Instead of “Precision is 0.82” → “Of those the model predicts as sick, 82% actually are sick.”

Whether a model is “good enough” depends entirely on the context and the consequences of errors. For a serious disease screening, low recall (missing sick patients) is unacceptable. For a less serious screening tool, decent precision might be sufficient.

title: Data Mining — Week 4 Notes
text:
3 ROC Curve and AUC

3.1 What Is the ROC Curve?
A Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve is a plot that shows how a binary classifier performs across all possible decision thresholds. Originally developed for military radar operators in World War II.

Y-axis: Sensitivity (same as Recall) — the true positive rate
X-axis: 1 − Specificity — the false positive rate, where Specificity = TN / (TN + FP)

A perfect classifier would have a curve that goes straight up from (0,0) to (0,1) and then across to (1,1), covering the entire top-left area.


3.2 Area Under the Curve (AUC)
The AUC is the area under the ROC curve, ranging from 0 to 1. A higher AUC indicates better model performance:

AUC = 1.0 → perfect classifier
AUC = 0.5 → no better than random guessing (the diagonal line)
AUC < 0.5 → worse than random (predictions are inverted)

In the Titanic example, the decision tree model had an AUC of 0.764.

title: Data Mining — Week 4 Notes
text:
4 Precision / Recall Trade-Off

4.1 Using Probabilities Instead of Hard Classes
Most ML models don’t just output “0” or “1” — they output a probability. For example, a model might say a passenger has a 0.63 probability of surviving and a 0.37 probability of not surviving.
We typically use a threshold of 0.5: if the probability of the positive class ≥ 0.5, predict Positive; otherwise, predict Negative.


4.2 Changing the Threshold
But there’s nothing magical about 0.5. By changing the threshold, we can shift the balance between precision and recall:

Lower the threshold (e.g., 0.1): The model predicts “Positive” more easily → higher recall (catches more true positives), but lower precision (more false positives). Good when you need to find as many positive cases as possible (e.g., fraud detection, disease screening).
Raise the threshold (e.g., 0.9): The model only predicts “Positive” when very confident → higher precision (fewer false alarms), but lower recall (misses more true positives). Good when you need high confidence in positive predictions.

Example from the lab (logistic regression on Titanic):



Threshold
Accuracy
Precision
Recall




0.5
73%
0.73
0.53


0.1
65%
0.54
0.92


0.9
64%
1.00
0.11



This is the fundamental precision/recall trade-off: improving one generally comes at the expense of the other.

title: Data Mining — Week 4 Notes
text:
5 Logistic Regression

5.1 What Is It?
Logistic regression is a classification algorithm (despite having “regression” in the name). It uses the mathematical framework of linear regression but adapts it for binary classification using the logistic (sigmoid) function.
The formula is: \(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \dots + \beta_n x_n\)
The result is passed through a sigmoid function to produce a probability between 0 and 1.


5.2 Key Points from Class

Coefficients are interpretable: Negative coefficients mean higher values of that feature decrease the probability of the positive class. For example, a negative coefficient for Sexmale means being male decreases the chance of survival.
Does not handle missing values: Unlike decision trees, logistic regression cannot process rows with NA values. If you include a column with missing data (like Age in the Titanic dataset), the predict() function returns NA for those rows. You must either drop those rows or impute the missing values (e.g., mean imputation).
Statistical significance of features: The summary() output shows p-values and significance codes (stars) for each feature. Features with no stars (high p-values) may not be contributing to the model and can be considered for removal.
Occam’s Razor in ML: A simpler model (fewer features) is preferable if it achieves comparable accuracy. In the lab, removing Embarked, Parch, and Fare actually improved accuracy slightly (from 73% to 74%), because those variables were either not significant or were correlated with other features already in the model.



5.3 Handling Categorical Variables
When logistic regression encounters a categorical variable (like Sex with values “male” and “female”), it automatically creates indicator variables. One category is absorbed into the intercept — in R, this is typically the first alphabetical value. So Sexmale appears as a coefficient, while the effect of being female is captured in the intercept.


5.4 Correlated Variables
A variable like Embarked may appear statistically insignificant not because it has no relationship with survival, but because its effect is already explained by other variables (like Pclass). This is called multicollinearity. When variables are correlated, the model says “I already have this information from another feature.”


5.5 Lab 4a — R Code Summary
# Logistic regression on Titanic
logistic_model <- glm(formula = Survived ~ .,
                      family = binomial(link = "logit"),
                      data = train_data)
summary(logistic_model)

# Get probabilities
pr <- predict(logistic_model, newdata = test_data, type = "response")

# Convert to classes using threshold
pr <- ifelse(pr > 0.5, 1, 0)

title: Data Mining — Week 4 Notes
text:
6 Evaluation Metrics for Regression
When the target variable is numeric (not a category), we use different metrics:

6.1 Mean Absolute Error (MAE)
\[\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} |y_i - \hat{y}_i|\]
The average absolute difference between predicted and actual values. Easy to interpret — if MAE is 15 and you’re predicting spending in euros, then predictions are off by €15 on average.


6.2 Mean Squared Error (MSE)
\[\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2\]
Squares the errors before averaging. Penalises large errors more heavily than MAE, because squaring amplifies big differences (an error of 5 contributes 25, while an error of 2 contributes only 4).


6.3 Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)
\[\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}\]
Takes the square root of MSE to bring the error back into the same units as the original values. RMSE will always be ≥ MAE. Preferred in many applications because it penalises large errors while remaining interpretable.


6.4 R-Squared (R²)
\[R^2 = (\text{Correlation}(y, \hat{y}))^2\]
Measures how much of the variability in the target can be explained by the model. Ranges from 0 to 1:

R² = 1 → model explains all variability (perfect fit)
R² = 0 → model explains none of the variability

For regression tasks, R² and RMSE are usually the preferred metrics.

title: Data Mining — Week 4 Notes
text:
7 The Bias-Variance Trade-Off
This is one of the most fundamental concepts in machine learning.

7.1 Bias (Underfitting)
Bias is the error from a model that is too simple — it cannot capture the underlying pattern in the data. A high-bias model performs poorly on both training and test data.
Think of it as a straight line trying to fit curved data — it’s too rigid and misses the pattern entirely.


7.2 Variance (Overfitting)
Variance is the error from a model that is too complex — it learns the noise and random fluctuations in the training data, not just the real pattern. A high-variance model performs well on training data but poorly on test data.
Think of it as a wiggly line that passes through every training point but goes wildly off-track for new data.


7.3 The Trade-Off
You cannot minimise both bias and variance simultaneously. Making a model more complex reduces bias but increases variance. Making it simpler increases bias but reduces variance. The goal is to find the sweet spot — a model complex enough to capture the real pattern but not so complex that it memorises noise.


7.4 Polynomial Regression Example (Lab 4b)
The lab demonstrated this trade-off using polynomial regression on synthetic data:










Model
Formula
R²
MAE
Behaviour




1
\(y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x\)
0.17
3.45
Underfits — straight line misses all patterns


2
\(y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \beta_2 x^2 + \beta_3 x^3\)
0.79
1.54
Good fit — captures the general trend


3
\(y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \dots + \beta_6 x^6\)
0.97
0.62
Overfits — looks great on training data but oscillates wildly outside the training range



Model 3 has the best metrics on training data, but it would perform terribly on new data because it has memorised the training noise.


7.5 How to Detect Overfitting

Compare performance on training data vs test data. If training performance is much better, the model is likely overfitting.
Change the random seed and rebuild the model. If accuracy varies wildly across different train/test splits, the model is not generalising well.
Cross-validation (covered in a future class) is a more systematic approach to this.

title: Data Mining — Week 4 Notes
text:
8 Survey of Other ML Algorithms

8.1 Naive Bayes
Based on: Bayesian statistics — updating beliefs with new evidence.
Core idea: Start with a prior belief (e.g., 38% of passengers survived), then update it with each piece of evidence (e.g., the passenger is female → higher survival probability, the passenger is 1st class → even higher probability).
\[\text{Posterior} = \frac{\text{Prior} \times \text{Likelihood}}{\text{Prior} \times \text{Likelihood} + (1 - \text{Prior}) \times (1 - \text{Likelihood})}\]
“Naive” because: It assumes all features are independent of each other, which is rarely true in practice (e.g., ticket class and fare are obviously related).
Advantages: Very fast and scalable — probabilities for each feature can be precomputed. Good as a baseline model.
Disadvantages: Rarely produces the best results because the independence assumption doesn’t hold in real data.
Titanic accuracy: ~72%


8.2 Support Vector Machines (SVM)
Core idea: Find the best boundary (hyperplane) that separates two classes with the maximum margin — the widest gap between the closest points of each class.
Two key elements:

Margin maximisation: Instead of just drawing any separating line, SVM finds the one that creates the widest possible gap between classes. The data points closest to the boundary are called support vectors.
The kernel trick: Maps data into a higher-dimensional space using kernel functions (similar in spirit to polynomial regression), allowing SVM to find non-linear boundaries.

Advantages: Good results without much tuning, robust to outliers (because the model only depends on support vectors near the boundary, not distant points).
Disadvantages: Does not scale well to very large datasets — can be very slow.
Titanic accuracy: ~76%


8.3 Neural Networks
Core idea: A network of interconnected nodes (neurons) organised in layers. Each node performs a simple mathematical function, and an activation function introduces non-linearity.
How training works:

Forward propagation: Input values pass through the network layer by layer, multiplied by weights, until they reach the output.
Cost function: The difference between the predicted output and the actual values is calculated.
Backward propagation: The weights are adjusted slightly, working backwards from the output, to reduce the error.
This cycle repeats hundreds or thousands of times until the error stops improving.

Important considerations:

Neural networks require numeric and scaled inputs. Categorical variables must be one-hot encoded, and numeric variables should be scaled to a 0–1 range.
They need a lot of data to perform well.
For standard tabular data, they typically don’t outperform simpler models like Random Forest or SVM. Their strength is in domains like computer vision and natural language processing.

Titanic accuracy: ~75–78%


8.4 Random Forest (Ensemble Method)
Core idea: Instead of building one decision tree, build hundreds of them, each trained on slightly different data and features, then take a majority vote.
This is grounded in the Central Limit Theorem: the average of many independent (even imperfect) estimates tends to be very accurate. Like asking 100 people to guess the number of marbles in a jar — individual guesses may be off by 20–30%, but the average is typically within 1–2%.
Three techniques to create diversity among the trees:

Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating): Each tree is trained on a random sample of rows drawn with replacement. About 63% of rows are unique in each sample; the rest are duplicates. The ~37% of rows not included are called “out-of-bag” (OOB) samples and can be used to estimate error.
Random feature selection: At each split in each tree, only a small random subset of features is considered. This prevents one dominant feature (e.g., gender in Titanic) from always being chosen first.
Shallow trees: Individual trees are kept small (depth 1–2) to avoid overfitting.

Why intentionally weaken the base models? Because we want diverse viewpoints on the data. If a few trees are poor, they get outvoted by the majority.
Advantages: Generally provides very good results without much tuning. Corrects for decision trees’ tendency to overfit. Provides feature importance rankings.
Disadvantages: Less interpretable than a single decision tree.
Titanic accuracy: ~78% (best among the models tested)
Feature importance from the Titanic Random Forest: Sex > Fare > Age > Pclass > SibSp > Parch > Embarked

title: Data Mining — Week 4 Notes
text:
9 Sampling: With vs Without Replacement
This concept is important for understanding how Random Forest works.
Without replacement (default): Once an item is selected, it cannot be selected again. Like drawing lottery numbers — each number is unique.
tmp <- sample(100, 90)           # Select 90 from 1:100
length(unique(tmp))              # All 90 are unique
With replacement: Items can be selected more than once. Like drawing a marble, noting it, putting it back, and drawing again.
tmp <- sample(90, 90, replace = TRUE)   # Select 90 from 1:90
length(unique(tmp))                      # ~57 unique (≈63%)
When sampling n items from n with replacement, approximately 63% will be unique. This is the basis of bagging in Random Forest.

title: Data Mining — Week 4 Notes
text:
10 Key Takeaways

Accuracy is misleading for imbalanced data or when error costs are unequal. Use precision, recall, and F1 instead.
Precision vs Recall is a trade-off — you can shift the balance by adjusting the probability threshold.
Always translate metrics into plain language when communicating with non-technical stakeholders.
Logistic regression is interpretable, fast, and a good baseline for classification — but cannot handle missing data.
Bias = underfitting (model too simple); Variance = overfitting (model too complex). The goal is the right balance.
Random Forest is typically one of the best-performing algorithms for tabular data out of the box.
Feature engineering (creating derived features) can improve models, but too much can cause overfitting.

title: Data Mining — Week 4 Notes
text:

title: Data Mining - Class 5 Notes
text:
Before diving into new material, this class revisited precision and recall — two key evaluation metrics for classification problems.


When a model makes predictions on a binary classification problem (e.g., survived/not survived, fraud/not fraud), there are four possible outcomes:

True Positive (TP): Model predicted positive, and it was actually positive.
True Negative (TN): Model predicted negative, and it was actually negative.
False Positive (FP): Model predicted positive, but it was actually negative.
False Negative (FN): Model predicted negative, but it was actually positive.

From these, we define:
\[
\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
\]
Recall (also called sensitivity) answers: “Of all the actual positive cases, how many did we find?” Think of it as the model’s ability to capture the positive cases.
\[
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
\]
Precision answers: “Of all the cases we predicted as positive, how many were actually positive?” Think of it as the quality of the positive predictions.



You typically cannot maximise both at the same time. By lowering the classification threshold (e.g., from 0.5 to 0.2), you capture more positive cases (higher recall), but you also incorrectly flag more negative cases as positive (lower precision).

High recall + low precision: You catch most positive cases, but many flagged cases are false alarms. Useful when missing a positive case is very costly (e.g., cancer screening), but you need a secondary filter to handle the false positives.
Low recall + high precision: You miss many positive cases, but the ones you do flag are almost certainly correct. This is less useful when missing positives has serious consequences.




A key lesson: most stakeholders don’t understand “precision” and “recall” directly. You should translate them into domain-specific language. For example, in a medical context:

Recall of 50% → “Of those who are sick, the model found 50% of them.”
Precision of 82% → “Of those the model predicted as sick, 82% actually were.”

Your job as a data scientist is to present these metrics clearly so decision-makers can judge whether the model is fit for purpose.

title: Data Mining - Class 5 Notes
text:
2 Linear Regression

2.1 What Is It?
Linear regression is one of the oldest and still most widely used machine learning algorithms. It predicts a continuous numeric value (y) as a linear combination of one or more input variables (x).
In its simplest form with one predictor:
\[
y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x
\]
Where:

\(\beta_0\) is the intercept (the value of y when x = 0)
\(\beta_1\) is the slope (how much y changes for each unit increase in x)

With multiple predictors, the formula extends to:
\[
y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \dots + \beta_n x_n
\]


2.2 How Are the Coefficients Calculated?
The “best” line is the one that minimises the residuals — the vertical distances between the actual data points and the predicted values on the line. The formulas for a simple (one-variable) case are:
\[
\beta_1 = \frac{\sum_i (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_i (x_i - \bar{x})^2}
\]
\[
\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}
\]
In R, the lm() function handles this automatically:


Code
library(openintro)
data(mammals)
model1 <- lm(body_wt ~ brain_wt, data = mammals)
print(model1$coefficients)


You can also verify by computing the coefficients manually:


Code
mean_x <- mean(mammals$brain_wt)
mean_y <- mean(mammals$body_wt)
numerator <- sum((mammals$brain_wt - mean_x) * (mammals$body_wt - mean_y))
denominator <- sum((mammals$brain_wt - mean_x) ^ 2)
b1 <- numerator / denominator
b0 <- mean_y - (b1 * mean_x)
sprintf("b0=%.2f, b1=%.2f", b0, b1)


Both approaches give the same result, confirming that regression is essentially a mathematical (matrix) calculation.


2.3 Assumptions of Linear Regression
Linear regression relies on several assumptions. If these are violated, the model may give misleading results:

Linearity: There is a linear relationship between predictors and the outcome. If not, you can apply transformations (e.g., log) or use polynomial terms.
Homoscedasticity: The spread (variance) of the residuals should be roughly constant across all levels of the predictor. In simple terms, the model should not be much more “wrong” for large values than for small values.
Independence: Each observation should be independent of the others. This can be violated with time-series data, where today’s value depends on yesterday’s.
Normality: The residuals (errors) should follow a roughly normal distribution.



2.4 Diagnostic Plots
After building a linear regression model, you should examine diagnostic plots to check the assumptions:
Residuals vs Fitted plot:

The x-axis shows predicted values; the y-axis shows errors (actual − predicted).
You want to see a random scatter with no pattern. A visible pattern (e.g., a funnel shape) suggests the model has problems.
The trend line should be approximately flat.

Normal Q-Q plot:

This checks whether residuals are normally distributed.
Points should fall along a straight diagonal line from bottom-left to top-right.
Deviations at the ends suggest non-normality or a non-linear relationship.



Code
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
plot(model1, which = 1, col = "blue")  # Residuals vs Fitted
plot(model1, which = 2, col = "blue")  # Normal Q-Q
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))




2.5 The Power of Data Transformation
Using the mammals dataset, the raw data (body weight vs brain weight) produces poor diagnostic plots because a few very large animals (elephants) dominate the data. The residuals show a clear pattern, and the Q-Q plot deviates badly.
By applying a log transformation to both variables, we get much better diagnostic plots — random residuals and a near-diagonal Q-Q plot. The R-squared also improves from 0.87 to 0.92 (a 35% reduction in error).


Code
mammals$log_body_wt <- log(mammals$body_wt)
mammals$log_brain_wt <- log(mammals$brain_wt)
model2 <- lm(log_body_wt ~ log_brain_wt, data = mammals)
summary(model2)


Key takeaway: Always examine your data distributions during exploratory analysis. If a variable is heavily skewed, a log transformation can dramatically improve your model.

title: Data Mining - Class 5 Notes
text:
3 Categorical Variables and One-Hot Encoding

3.1 The Problem
Many machine learning algorithms (including regression) work only with numeric data. But datasets often contain categorical (text/factor) variables like gender, ticket class, or embarkation port. We need a way to convert these into numbers.


3.2 What Is One-Hot Encoding?
One-hot encoding creates a new binary column for each unique value in a categorical variable. For each row, one of these new columns gets a 1 (the value that applies), and all others get 0.
Example:



id
gender
mood
outcome




1
male
happy
1


2
female
sad
1


3
female
happy
0



After one-hot encoding becomes:



id
gender.female
gender.male
mood.happy
mood.sad
outcome




1
0
1
1
0
1


2
1
0
0
1
1


3
1
0
1
0
0



In R, you can do this with the caret package:


Code
library(caret)
customers <- data.frame(
  id = c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5),
  gender = c('male', 'female', 'female', 'male', 'female'),
  mood = c('happy', 'sad', 'happy', 'sad', 'happy'),
  outcome = c(1, 1, 0, 0, 0))

dmy <- dummyVars(" ~ .", data = customers)
trsf <- data.frame(predict(dmy, newdata = customers))
head(trsf)




3.3 How R Handles It Automatically in GLM
When you pass a factor or character variable to glm() in R, it automatically does one-hot encoding behind the scenes. It creates N−1 dummy variables (one fewer than the number of unique values), and the “missing” category becomes the reference category, absorbed into the intercept.
For example, with Pclass (1, 2, 3): R creates Pclass2 and Pclass3. First class becomes the reference, absorbed into the intercept.


3.4 Factor vs Numeric: Why It Matters
A critical point: Pclass is coded as 1, 2, 3 — but it is NOT a numeric variable. You cannot meaningfully subtract a 3rd class ticket from a 2nd class ticket to get a 1st class ticket. It is a categorical variable.
The test: Can you do meaningful arithmetic on the values? If not, treat it as a factor.


Code
titanic_train$Pclass <- as.factor(titanic_train$Pclass)


Without this conversion, R treats Pclass as a single numeric predictor (implying that the difference between 1st and 2nd class is the same as between 2nd and 3rd), which is incorrect.

title: Data Mining - Class 5 Notes
text:
4 Logistic Regression

4.1 From Numbers to Categories
Linear regression predicts a continuous number. But for classification (survived/not survived), we need to predict a probability between 0 and 1. This is where logistic regression comes in.


4.2 Step 1: Calculate the Log Odds
First, logistic regression computes a linear combination of the features, just like linear regression:
\[
\text{log\_odds} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \dots + \beta_n x_n
\]
This value can range from \(-\infty\) to \(+\infty\).


4.3 Step 2: Apply the Sigmoid Function
To convert the log odds into a probability between 0 and 1, we apply the sigmoid function:
\[
p = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\text{log\_odds}}}
\]
This function has an S-shape:

Very negative inputs → probability near 0
Input of 0 → probability of exactly 0.5
Very positive inputs → probability near 1



Code
x <- seq(-5, 5, by = 0.01)
y <- 1 / (1 + exp(-x))
plot(x, y, main = "Sigmoid function", col = "blue")




4.4 Step 3: Apply a Threshold
Once you have a probability, you apply a threshold (typically 0.5) to classify:

Probability > 0.5 → predict class 1 (survived)
Probability ≤ 0.5 → predict class 0 (not survived)

You can adjust this threshold to trade off precision and recall as discussed earlier.


4.5 Building a Logistic Regression Model in R


Code
library(titanic)
data(titanic_train)
titanic_train$Pclass <- as.factor(titanic_train$Pclass)

set.seed(42)
nobs <- nrow(titanic_train)
train <- sample(nobs, 0.9 * nobs)
test <- setdiff(seq_len(nobs), train)

target <- "Survived"
colsToIgnore <- c("PassengerId", "Name", "Cabin", "Ticket", "Age", "Parch", "Embarked")
input <- setdiff(names(titanic_train), c(target, colsToIgnore))

train_data <- titanic_train[train, c(input, target)]
test_data <- titanic_train[test, c(input, target)]

logistic_model <- glm(formula = Survived ~ .,
                      family = binomial(link = "logit"),
                      data = train_data)

pr <- predict(logistic_model, newdata = test_data, type = "response")
pr <- ifelse(pr > 0.5, 1, 0)




4.6 Interpreting the Coefficients
The summary() output gives you:

Estimate (coefficients): The \(\beta\) values. A positive coefficient means higher values of that feature increase the probability of the positive class (survival). A negative coefficient decreases it.
Pr(>|z|) (p-values): The statistical significance. You want these to be as low as possible. The significance codes (***, **, *, .) give a quick visual guide.

Important nuances:

The intercept absorbs the reference categories of all factor variables (e.g., female, 1st class).
You cannot directly compare coefficient magnitudes across features unless the features are scaled, because they depend on the unit of measurement. For instance, Sexmale ranges 0–1 while Fare ranges 0–500+.
Variables with low statistical significance and small coefficients (like Fare in this model) are candidates for removal — their effect is likely captured by other variables (like Pclass).



4.7 Verifying Predictions Manually
One of the great advantages of logistic regression is that you can reproduce predictions without the predict function, just by plugging coefficients into the formula and applying the sigmoid:


Code
coeff <- logistic_model$coefficients

# Calculate log odds manually
test_data$log_odds <- coeff[1] +
  coeff[2] * ifelse(test_data$Sex == "male", 1, 0) +
  coeff[3] * test_data$SibSp +
  coeff[4] * test_data$Fare +
  coeff[5] * ifelse(test_data$Pclass == "2", 1, 0) +
  coeff[6] * ifelse(test_data$Pclass == "3", 1, 0)

# Apply sigmoid
test_data$probability <- 1 / (1 + exp(-test_data$log_odds))


This is practical because it means you can deploy a logistic regression model in any environment (SQL, JavaScript, Python, etc.) without needing R’s predict function.

title: Data Mining - Class 5 Notes
text:
5 Feature Engineering (Lab Work)
Feature engineering is the process of creating, transforming, or selecting features to improve model performance. This was the main hands-on activity of the lab.

5.1 The Iterative Process
The approach followed in class was:

Build a baseline model with available features.
Examine the summary() output — look at coefficients and p-values.
Remove the single least significant variable (highest p-value).
Rebuild the model and check if accuracy holds or improves.
Repeat until all remaining features are statistically significant.
Then explore whether new features can be engineered from existing data.

Important rule: Only remove one variable at a time, because removing one can change the significance of others (due to collinearity).
This process is sometimes called stepwise regression.


5.2 Handling Missing Values: Mean Imputation
The Age variable had missing values. Initially, these were filled with the overall mean age:


Code
meanAge <- mean(train_data$Age, na.rm = TRUE)
titanic_train[is.na(titanic_train$Age), ]$Age <- meanAge


Later, a smarter imputation was developed using title and ticket class (see below).


5.3 Feature Engineering Experiments
Several experiments were tried on the Titanic dataset:
Removing insignificant variables (Parch, Fare, Embarked): Removing these one by one actually improved accuracy from ~73% to ~76%. Simpler models with only significant features often perform better.
Converting Age to a binary flag (AgeOver15): Testing whether being a child matters more than exact age. The flag was significant, but didn’t improve accuracy compared to using raw age.
Extracting Deck from Cabin: The first letter of the Cabin field represents the deck. This was extracted and tested, but none of the deck categories were statistically significant, so it was dropped.
Extracting Title from Name: The Name field contains titles (Mr., Mrs., Miss, Master, etc.). These were extracted using a regular expression and rationalised into four categories: Mr, Mrs, Miss, and Master (xMaster).


Code
# Extract title from name
titanic_train$Title <- gsub('(.*, )|(\\..*)', '', titanic_train$Name)

# Rationalise rare titles
titanic_train$Title[titanic_train$Title %in% c("Mlle", "Ms")] <- "Miss"
titanic_train$Title[titanic_train$Title %in% c("Mme")] <- "Mrs"
titanic_train$Title[titanic_train$Title %in% c("Capt", "Rev", "Col",
  "Don", "Major", "Sir", "Dr", "Jonkheer")] <- "Mr"
titanic_train$Title[titanic_train$Title == "the Countess"] <- "Mrs"
titanic_train$Title[titanic_train$Title == "Lady"] <- "Mrs"


Smarter Age Imputation using Title and Pclass: Instead of filling missing ages with the global mean, the mean age was calculated per group (Title × Pclass). This means a missing age for a “Master” in 3rd class gets a value around 5 (child), while a missing “Mr.” in 1st class gets around 42. This improved the model’s accuracy.


5.4 Final Model
After all the feature engineering, the final model used: Pclass, Sex, SibSp, and AgeOver15 (or Age), achieving approximately 75.5% accuracy with all features statistically significant.
Key lesson: Feature engineering often matters more than choosing a fancier algorithm. Understanding your data and creating meaningful features from existing information is one of the most valuable skills in data science.

title: Data Mining - Class 5 Notes
text:
6 Summary
This class covered:







Topic
Key Idea




Precision & Recall
Trade-off between catching all positives vs quality of positive predictions


Linear Regression
Predict a number as a weighted sum of inputs; check assumptions with diagnostic plots


Data Transformation
Log transforms can fix non-linear relationships and improve models dramatically


One-Hot Encoding
Convert categorical variables to numeric binary columns for regression models


Factor Variables
Numeric-looking variables (like Pclass) may need to be treated as factors, not numbers


Logistic Regression
Linear regression + sigmoid function = probability-based classification


Sigmoid Function
Maps any number to a probability between 0 and 1


Interpreting Coefficients
Sign = direction of effect; magnitude depends on scale; p-value = significance


Stepwise Feature Removal
Remove least significant features one at a time to simplify and improve models


Feature Engineering
Extract hidden information (titles from names), improve imputations, create new variables

text:
7 Recommended Reading

“An Introduction to Statistical Learning” (ISLR) — Chapter 3 on Linear Regression. Freely available at: https://www.statlearning.com/

title: Data Mining - Class 5 Notes
text:
8 My Notes

Add your own observations, questions, and additional insights here as you review the material.

title: Week 2 - Statistical Inference + Missing Data
text:
This class is a refresher of statistical inference + practical R work with missing data.
Think of it like this:

Theory → How we make decisions from data
R → How we actually apply this in real datasets

👉 The key idea of this lecture is:

We use a sample to make decisions about a population, but there is always uncertainty.

title: Week 2 - Statistical Inference + Missing Data
text:
1. Population vs Sample
From the slides:

Population → all individuals we are interested in (true but unknown)
Sample → the data we actually collect
Parameter (μ) → true value from the population (we never know this)
Statistic (x̄) → estimate from the sample


🧠 How to think about it
You can think of it like:

Population = the truth
Sample = a guess based on limited data

👉 Example:

We want average height of all adults in Ireland (population)
We measure 100 people (sample)
We calculate mean → this is our estimate

✔ Important:

All statistical inference is about using statistics (sample) to estimate parameters (population)

title: Week 2 - Statistical Inference + Missing Data
text:
2. Sampling Error
Sampling error is the difference between the sample estimate and the true population value.

Example

True mean = 100
Sample mean = 95 → Error = 5



🧠 Why this happens
Because:

We are not observing everyone
Each sample is slightly different

👉 If you took another sample, you would get a different mean.



How to reduce sampling error

Larger sample size → more representative
Random sampling → avoids bias
Confidence intervals → quantify uncertainty

✔ Key idea:

Sampling error is unavoidable, but we can reduce it

title: Week 2 - Statistical Inference + Missing Data
text:
3. Sampling Distribution
This is one of the most important concepts in statistics.

🧠 Idea
Imagine you:

Take a sample
Calculate the mean
Repeat this MANY times

You would get many different sample means.
👉 These means form a distribution → called the sampling distribution



Key properties

Mean of sampling distribution = population mean (μ)
Spread = standard error (SE)




🧠 Why this matters
This is the foundation of:

Confidence intervals
Hypothesis testing

👉 Because it tells us:

How much our estimate can vary

title: Week 2 - Statistical Inference + Missing Data
text:
Central Limit Theorem (CLT)
Even if the original data is not normal:
If ( n ), the distribution of the sample mean becomes approximately normal


🧠 Why this is powerful
It allows us to:

Use normal distribution formulas
Apply inference methods in real-world messy data

👉 Without CLT → most statistics wouldn’t work

title: Week 2 - Statistical Inference + Missing Data
text:
4. Standard Error (VERY IMPORTANT)
\[
SE = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
\]
In practice:
\[
SE = \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}
\]


🧠 What it means

SE measures how much the sample mean varies
It is the uncertainty of our estimate




🧠 Intuition

Large n → √n increases → SE decreases
Small SE → more precise estimate

✔ Key idea:

Bigger samples → more reliable results

title: Week 2 - Statistical Inference + Missing Data
text:
1. Hypotheses

H₀ (null) → no effect / no difference
H₁ (alternative) → effect exists



🧠 Key idea
We start by assuming H₀ is true, and then test if data contradicts it.
👉 Example:
\[
H_0: \mu = 0
\]
\[
H_1: \mu \neq 0
\]



Important rule
The hypotheses must cover all possibilities:

Either there is an effect
Or there is not

title: Week 2 - Statistical Inference + Missing Data
text:
2. What are we actually testing?
We are asking:

“How likely is our data if H₀ is true?”

title: Week 2 - Statistical Inference + Missing Data
text:
3. p-value (CRITICAL)

Definition
The p-value is:

The probability of observing our data (or more extreme), assuming H₀ is true




🧠 How to interpret

Small p-value → data is unlikely under H₀
Large p-value → data is consistent with H₀




Decision rule

If p ≤ 0.05 → reject H₀
If p > 0.05 → fail to reject H₀




⚠️ Important wording (exam!)
✔ Say:

“Reject H₀”
“Fail to reject H₀”

❌ Do NOT say:

“Accept H₀”




🧠 Key intuition
👉 “If p is low, H₀ must go”

title: Week 2 - Statistical Inference + Missing Data
text:
4. Statistical Significance

Means there is evidence of an effect
Does NOT mean the effect is important

title: Week 3 - Power Analysis + Missing Data
text:
This class had two main parts:

Power analysis → how likely a statistical test is to detect a real effect
Missing data → how to identify, visualise, and deal with missing values in R

These two topics are very important in real data analysis because they answer two practical questions:

Do we have enough data to detect something important?
Can we trust our analysis if some of the data is missing?

A lot of statistics courses focus on how to run a test, but this class goes one step further. It asks whether the test had a fair chance of finding an effect in the first place, and whether missing values may be distorting the result.

title: Week 3 - Power Analysis + Missing Data
text:
1. What is statistical power?
Statistical power is the probability of rejecting the null hypothesis when the null hypothesis is false.
In symbols:
\[
\text{Power} = 1 - \beta
\]
where:

( ) = probability of a Type II error
( 1 - ) = probability of correctly detecting a real effect


How to think about it
Power is basically the test’s ability to notice something real.
Imagine a smoke alarm:

A powerful smoke alarm goes off when there really is smoke
A weak smoke alarm misses the smoke and stays silent

A statistical test is similar. If there really is an effect in the population, a high-power test is more likely to find it.

title: Week 3 - Power Analysis + Missing Data
section: 2. Why do we need power analysis?
text:
2. Why do we need power analysis?
A non-significant result can mean two different things:

there really is no effect
there is an effect, but the study was too weak to detect it

This is why power matters.

Real-world meaning
Suppose a new medicine really helps patients a little, but your sample is tiny.
Your test may say:

“Fail to reject the null hypothesis”

But that does not necessarily mean the medicine does nothing. It may just mean the study did not have enough power.
That is the main purpose of power analysis:

to check whether the sample size is large enough to detect an effect that matters

title: Week 3 - Power Analysis + Missing Data
text:
3. The four linked quantities
The slides say that four quantities are related:

sample size (n)
effect size
significance level ( )
power (1 - )


Why this matters
These four quantities work like a balancing system.
If you change one, the others are affected.


General pattern

larger sample size → higher power
larger effect size → higher power
smaller alpha (stricter test) → lower power
higher desired power → usually need larger sample size



Intuition
If you are trying to detect a very small difference, you need more data.
If you are happy to detect only large differences, you may need less data.
So power analysis is really about matching the study design to the kind of effect you care about.

title: Week 3 - Power Analysis + Missing Data
text:
4. Effect size in power analysis
In practice, we do not know the true effect size.
If we already knew the true effect, there would be no need to do the test.
So instead, we choose a minimum effect size of practical importance.

Important idea
This is not just “any difference”.
It is the smallest difference that would actually matter in real life.


Real-world example from the lecture
The slides give an example with premature infants, where a difference of 500 g in weight gain was considered clinically significant.
That means the researchers were saying:

“We are not just interested in any tiny difference. We want to be able to detect a difference of at least 500 g, because that is the minimum difference that matters in practice.”

This is the difference between:

statistical significance → is there evidence of some effect?
practical or clinical significance → is the effect large enough to matter?

title: Week 3 - Power Analysis + Missing Data
text:
5. Chemical company example
The lecture uses a good example:
A company claims that the mean amount of caustic material is:
\[
\mu = 4.0
\]
An inspector takes a sample of 30 containers and gets:

sample mean ( {x} = 4.3 )
known standard deviation ( = 1.3 )

If the true amount is greater than 4, the product may be dangerous.

title: Week 3 - Power Analysis + Missing Data
text:
6. Step 1 - State the hypotheses
Because the product is only dangerous if the mean is greater than 4, this is a one-tailed test.
\[
H_0: \mu = 4.0
\]
\[
H_A: \mu > 4.0
\]

Why one-tailed?
Because we only care about too much caustic material.
If the mean were lower than 4, that is not the danger being studied here.

title: Week 3 - Power Analysis + Missing Data
section: 7. Step 2 - Type I and Type II errors in context
text:
7. Step 2 - Type I and Type II errors in context

Type I error
Reject (H_0) when it is actually true.
In this problem:

We conclude that the true mean is greater than 4 ml per litre when in fact it is not.

So we would falsely label the product as dangerous.



Type II error
Fail to reject (H_0) when it is actually false.
In this problem:

We conclude that the true mean is not greater than 4 ml per litre when in fact it is.

So we would miss a dangerous product.


Why Type II error matters here
This is why power analysis is important.
In safety settings, failing to detect a real danger can be a serious problem.

title: Week 3 - Power Analysis + Missing Data
section: 8. Step 3 - Carry out the hypothesis test
text:
8. Step 3 - Carry out the hypothesis test
Since the population standard deviation is known, we use a one-sample Z test.
The test statistic is:
\[
Z = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}
\]
Substitute the values:
\[
Z = \frac{4.3 - 4.0}{1.3 / \sqrt{30}} = 1.264
\]
At the 5% significance level for a one-tailed test, the critical value is:
\[
Z_{crit} = 1.645
\]
Since:
\[
1.264 < 1.645
\]
we fail to reject the null hypothesis.

Conclusion
The data does not provide enough evidence to conclude that the mean level of caustic material is greater than 4 ml per litre.


Important interpretation
This does not prove the mean is 4 or less.
It only means the sample did not give strong enough evidence to conclude it is greater than 4.
That distinction is very important.

title: Week 3 - Power Analysis + Missing Data
text:
9. p-value and lower confidence bound
The lecture also gives:

( p = 0.103 )
95% lower confidence bound = 3.910

Since ( p > 0.05 ), the result is not statistically significant.
The lower confidence bound means:

We are 95% confident that the true population mean is greater than 3.910.


Why this is useful
The p-value only helps with the decision.
The confidence bound gives more information about the range of plausible values.

title: Week 3 - Power Analysis + Missing Data
text:
10. The key power question
After failing to reject the null, the lecturer asks the really important question:

Did the result show there is no dangerous increase, or was the sample just too small to detect it?

That is exactly what power analysis helps answer.

title: Week 3 - Power Analysis + Missing Data
section: 11. Calculating power for a meaningful effect
text:
11. Calculating power for a meaningful effect
Suppose we decide that a dangerous increase would be:
\[
4.35 \text{ ml per litre}
\]
That means the effect size of interest is:
\[
0.35
\]
Now the question becomes:

If the true mean really were 4.35, how likely would this test be to detect it?

That probability is the power.

title: Week 3 - Power Analysis + Missing Data
section: 12. Rejection rule in terms of the sample mean
text:
12. Rejection rule in terms of the sample mean
Earlier we had the critical Z value:
\[
Z_{crit} = 1.645
\]
We can convert this back to the sample-mean scale:
\[
\bar{X} = \mu + \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} Z_{crit}
\]
Substituting:
\[
\bar{X} = 4 + \frac{1.3}{\sqrt{30}}(1.645) = 4.39
\]

Interpretation

If ( {X} > 4.39 ) → reject (H_0)
If ( {X} < 4.39 ) → fail to reject (H_0)

So the sample mean must be at least 4.39 for the test to detect the effect.


Intuition
This is the “cut-off point” needed before the evidence is strong enough.

title: Week 3 - Power Analysis + Missing Data
section: 13. Calculating Type II error and power
text:
13. Calculating Type II error and power
If the true mean is actually (4.35), then Type II error means:

we fail to reject the null even though the alternative is true

So we need:
\[
P(\bar{X} < 4.39 \mid \mu = 4.35)
\]
Convert to a Z score:
\[
Z = \frac{4.39 - 4.35}{1.3/\sqrt{30}} = 0.17
\]
From the normal table:
\[
P(Z < 0.17) = 0.5675
\]
So:
\[
\beta = 0.5675
\]
and therefore:
\[
\text{Power} = 1 - \beta = 1 - 0.5675 = 0.4325
\]
So the power is:
\[
43.25\%
\]

title: Week 3 - Power Analysis + Missing Data
text:
14. What does power = 43.25% mean?
This means:

If the true mean is really 4.35, this test would detect that effect only about 43% of the time.

That is not very good.

Interpretation
There is a bigger chance of missing the effect than detecting it.
So this low power suggests the study may be too weak to reliably detect that effect.


Real-world meaning
If you are testing something potentially dangerous, 43% power is poor.
You would want a better chance of catching the problem.
Usually researchers want power around 80% or higher, though the lecture did not focus on that threshold explicitly.

title: Week 3 - Power Analysis + Missing Data
section: 15. Why smaller effects are harder to detect
text:
15. Why smaller effects are harder to detect
The exercise then changes the effect size from 0.35 to 0.1.
The answer becomes much lower power.

Why?
Because smaller effects are harder to separate from random noise.


Real-world analogy
It is easier to hear a loud alarm in a noisy room than a very quiet beep.
A large effect stands out more clearly. A small effect gets lost more easily.

title: Week 3 - Power Analysis + Missing Data
text:
16. Confidence interval formula
When the population standard deviation is known, the confidence interval for a mean is:
\[
\bar{x} \pm Z_{\alpha/2} \times \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
\]
The term
\[
Z_{\alpha/2} \times \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
\]
is the margin of error (ME).

title: Week 3 - Power Analysis + Missing Data
text:
17. What is margin of error?
The margin of error is how far we are willing for our estimate to be from the true value.

Intuition
If the margin of error is small, we want a very precise estimate.
If we want a more precise estimate, we usually need a larger sample size.
So margin of error and sample size are directly connected.

title: Week 3 - Power Analysis + Missing Data
section: 18. Sample size formula from margin of error
text:
18. Sample size formula from margin of error
Starting with:
\[
ME = Z_{\alpha/2} \times \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
\]
rearranging gives:
\[
n = \left(\frac{Z_{\alpha/2}\sigma}{ME}\right)^2
\]

What this means
If you know:

your confidence level
your standard deviation
your desired margin of error

you can work out how much data you need.

title: Week 3 - Power Analysis + Missing Data
text:
19. Sample size example
Suppose an analyst wants to estimate mean voltage.
Given:

population standard deviation ( = 22.5 )
margin of error ( ME = 5 )
95% confidence level, so ( Z = 1.96 )

Then:
\[
n = \left(\frac{1.96 \times 22.5}{5}\right)^2
\]
This gives:
\[
n = 77.8
\]
Since sample size must be a whole number, we round up:
\[
n = 78
\]

Why round up?
Because 77 would not quite give the precision we need.
When planning sample size, always round up.

title: Week 3 - Power Analysis + Missing Data
text:
20. Unknown standard deviation
In real life, the population standard deviation is often unknown.
Then we use the sample standard deviation (s) instead, and the distribution is based on t rather than Z.
That is more realistic, but also a little more complicated.
The main idea is still the same:

more precision requires more data

title: Week 3 - Power Analysis + Missing Data
text:
21. Why missing data matters
Missing data is not just annoying. It can change your results.
If missing values are ignored carelessly, you may:

lose a lot of data
reduce sample size
reduce power
introduce bias
fit models on a non-representative subset

So missing data is really about data quality, not just cleanup.

title: Week 3 - Power Analysis + Missing Data
section: 22. Useful R functions for small amounts of missing data
text:
22. Useful R functions for small amounts of missing data
The slides list several useful functions when there are only a few missing values and no obvious missing-data pattern.

is.na(x)
Checks whether each value is missing.
It returns:

TRUE if missing
FALSE if not missing

### `is.na(x)`
Checks whether each value is missing.
It returns:

TRUE if missing
FALSE if not missing

is.na(x)


🧠 How to think about it
This is the basic building block for missing data.
👉 You are asking:

“Where exactly is my data missing?”




sum(is.na(x))
Counts the number of missing values.
sum(is.na(x))


🧠 Why this is useful
Instead of looking at TRUE/FALSE values, this gives you a quick summary.
👉 Example:

If result = 5 → there are 5 missing values




mean(is.na(x))
Gives the proportion of missing values.
mean(is.na(x))


🧠 Why this matters

If result = 0.10 → 10% of data is missing
This helps you judge how serious the problem is




complete.cases(x)
Returns rows with no missing values.
complete.cases(x)



Removing missing data
x_clean <- x[complete.cases(x), ]



🧠 Important warning
This method is called listwise deletion.
It is simple, but:

reduces sample size
reduces power
may introduce bias

👉 If missing data is not random, this can be very dangerous.

title: Week 3 - Power Analysis + Missing Data
text:
23. Types of missing data (VERY IMPORTANT)
Not all missing data is the same.
This is critical for your understanding.


1. MCAR — Missing Completely At Random
Missingness has no relationship with any variable.
👉 Example:

A survey page is accidentally lost

✔ Safe to remove data (usually)



2. MAR — Missing At Random
Missingness depends on observed variables
👉 Example:

Income missing more often for younger people

✔ Can be handled with proper methods (e.g. imputation)



3. MNAR — Missing Not At Random
Missingness depends on the missing value itself
👉 Example:

People with very high income choose not to report it

❗ Most problematic case



🧠 Why this matters
👉 Because:

The method you choose depends on why the data is missing

title: Week 3 - Power Analysis + Missing Data
text:
24. Visualising missing data
The lecture uses the VIM package.
This is VERY useful in practice.


aggr() function
library(VIM)
aggr(data)



What it shows

Amount of missing data per variable
Patterns of missingness




🧠 How to interpret

Bars → how much is missing
Colours:

Blue = observed
Red = missing


👉 Helps answer:

“Which variables have the most missing data?”




barMiss()
barMiss(data)



What it does
Shows missing values grouped by categories
👉 Example:

Missing income by gender




🧠 Why this is powerful
Helps detect patterns
👉 If missing data is not random → this will show it



Scatter plots with missing data

Red points = missing values
Boxplots show distribution

👉 Helps understand:

Is missingness related to other variables?

title: Week 3 - Power Analysis + Missing Data
section: 25. What to do with missing data?
text:
25. What to do with missing data?
There is no single answer.
But here are common approaches:


1. Remove data (simple approach)
✔ Easy ❌ Can lose a lot of information



2. Imputation (fill in missing values)
Examples:

Mean imputation
Median imputation

👉 Replace missing values with a reasonable estimate



⚠️ Important limitation

Reduces variability
Can distort relationships




3. More advanced methods (not covered deeply yet)

Multiple imputation
Model-based methods

👉 You will likely see these later in the course

title: Week 3 - Power Analysis + Missing Data
section: 26. Key connection: Missing data and power
text:
26. Key connection: Missing data and power
This is VERY important and often overlooked.
👉 Missing data → smaller effective sample size
Which means:

higher standard error
wider confidence intervals
lower power



🧠 Big picture insight
Missing data is not just a “data cleaning issue”.
It directly affects:

inference
hypothesis testing
reliability of conclusions

title: Week 3 - Power Analysis + Missing Data
text:
Power Analysis

Power = probability of detecting a real effect
Depends on:

sample size
effect size
significance level


👉 Low power = high chance of missing real effects

title: Week 3 - Power Analysis + Missing Data
text:
Confidence and Sample Size

Margin of error controls precision
Larger sample → smaller error
Can calculate required sample size before study

title: Week 3 - Power Analysis + Missing Data
text:
Missing Data

Always check it first
Understand why it is missing
Be careful when removing data

title: Week 3 - Power Analysis + Missing Data
section: 🧠 Most important idea from this lecture
text:
🧠 Most important idea from this lecture

A non-significant result does NOT mean “no effect”

It could mean:

no effect
OR not enough power
OR poor data (missing values)
