text: The main takeaway from this class is that we just need to make sure that I understand what it is that you need to look at when you’re looking at each one because we will need to be able to interpret them.

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text: Note to self: These are summary notes from Class 3. Add your own observations, questions, and extra examples as you review the material.

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
3.1 What is it?
The Titanic dataset is one of the most well-known datasets in machine learning. It records information about passengers aboard the RMS Titanic, which sank in April 1912. The central question is:

Can we build a model that predicts which passengers survived the Titanic disaster?

This is a binary classification problem — for each passenger, the model must predict one of two outcomes: survived (1) or did not survive (0).

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
3.2 Variables in the Dataset








Variable
Description
Notes




PassengerId
Unique ID for each passenger
Not useful for prediction


Survived
0 = No, 1 = Yes
Target variable


Pclass
Ticket class: 1 = 1st, 2 = 2nd, 3 = 3rd
Very important predictor


Name
Passenger’s full name
Unique — drop it


Sex
Gender
Very strong predictor


Age
Age in years
Has many missing values


SibSp
# of siblings/spouses aboard
Combined with Parch


Parch
# of parents/children aboard
Combined with SibSp


Ticket
Ticket number
Likely not useful as-is


Fare
Price paid for the ticket
Correlated with Pclass


Cabin
Cabin number
Lots of missing values


Embarked
Port of embarkation: C, Q, or S
Some predictive signal

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
3.3 Loading the Data in R
library(tidyverse)
library(psych)

# Install the titanic package if not available
if (!require("titanic"))
  devtools::install_github("paulhendricks/titanic")
library("titanic")

data(titanic_train)
str(titanic_train)

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
4.1 Why Explore Data Before Modelling?
Data exploration is a critical step before building any machine learning model. It helps you:

Understand what the data looks like
Identify missing values and decide how to handle them
Spot variables that are likely to be useful predictors
Detect relationships between predictor variables (not just between each variable and the target)
Avoid mistakes that would silently damage your model’s quality


Rule of thumb: Always know your data before you model it. A model built on poorly understood data will produce unreliable results.

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
section: 4.2 Checking Unique Values and Missing Data
text:
4.2 Checking Unique Values and Missing Data
# Count unique values per column
uniq_len <- function(x) { length(unique(x)) }
sapply(titanic_train, uniq_len)

# Count missing values (NAs or empty strings)
countBlank <- function(x) { sum(is.na(x) | x == "") }
sapply(titanic_train, countBlank)
What to look for:

Columns with as many unique values as rows (e.g. PassengerId, Name) → These are IDs, not features. Drop them.
Columns with too many missing values (e.g. Cabin has 687 missing out of 891) → Consider dropping or imputing.
Unexpected number of unique values (e.g. Embarked shows 4 unique values when only 3 are expected) → Signals there are blank/null entries that need fixing.

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
section: 4.3 Key Findings from the Titanic Data
text:
4.3 Key Findings from the Titanic Data

4.3.1 Family Size vs. Survival
A new variable family is created by adding SibSp + Parch to represent total family members travelling together.
titanic_train$family <- titanic_train$SibSp + titanic_train$Parch
titanic_train$Survived <- factor(titanic_train$Survived)

ggplot(titanic_train, aes(x = family, fill = factor(Survived))) +
  geom_bar(stat = 'count', position = 'dodge') +
  labs(x = 'Family Size') +
  ggtitle("Family size v Survived")
Finding: Solo travellers had the lowest survival rate. Passengers in small family groups (1–3 others) had noticeably higher survival rates. Very large families (6+) had very few records, so conclusions there are not reliable.
Interpretation: Being in a family likely means children were present, and children were given priority in lifeboats.


4.3.2 Ticket Class vs. Survival
p01 <- ggplot(titanic_train, aes(Pclass, fill = Survived)) +
  geom_bar(position = "stack") + ggtitle("By counts")

p02 <- ggplot(titanic_train, aes(Pclass, fill = Survived)) +
  geom_bar(position = "fill") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) + ggtitle("By proportion")

gridExtra::grid.arrange(p01, p02, nrow = 1, top = 'Survived by Class')
Finding:

1st class: ~63% survived
2nd class: ~47% survived
3rd class: ~24% survived

Important tip — plot both counts AND proportions:

Counts tell you how many passengers are in each group (the effect size)
Proportions tell you how different the survival rate is from the baseline of 38% (the signal strength)

Both matter. A strong signal (e.g. 90% survival) in a group with only 10 records is not very useful — it barely affects the overall model.


4.3.3 Gender vs. Survival
Finding:

Female passengers: ~74% survived
Male passengers: ~19% survived

Gender is the single strongest predictor in this dataset. This aligns with the historical “women and children first” policy used when loading lifeboats.


4.3.4 Fixing the Embarked Variable
Two passengers had blank values for Embarked. Since Southampton was by far the most common embarkation port, those blanks were reassigned to S.
titanic_train[titanic_train$Embarked == "", ]$Embarked <- 'S'
Why not create a new “Unknown” category? Because with only 2 records, assigning them a unique value X could cause the model to over-fit on that tiny group. The safer choice is to merge them into the most common category.


4.3.5 Fare vs. Survival
A box plot of Fare by Survived shows that passengers who paid higher fares had a somewhat higher survival rate. However, fare is closely correlated with ticket class — they carry overlapping information.


4.3.6 Age vs. Survival — Creating a Derived Variable
A simple box plot of Age vs. Survived doesn’t show a strong difference. This is because the real signal is not about age in general, but specifically about children.
temp <- titanic_train[!is.na(titanic_train$Age), ]
temp$is_child <- ifelse(temp$Age < 17, 1, 0)
Children (under 17) had roughly a 50% survival rate vs. about 35% for adults. Creating a binary is_child variable helps the machine learning algorithm pick up on this threshold effect.

Key concept — derived variables: Sometimes the raw variable is not as useful as a transformed or binned version of it. Think about what threshold or transformation would best capture the relationship with the target.



4.3.7 Pairs Plot
columns <- c("Pclass", "Age", "Fare", "family")
pairs.panels(titanic_train[, columns], method = "pearson",
             hist.col = "#00AFBB", density = TRUE, ellipses = FALSE)
Finding: The strongest correlation among predictors is between Pclass and Fare (–0.55), which makes sense — first class passengers paid much more.
Why this matters: If two predictor variables are highly correlated, they carry redundant information. The model may just pick one of them and largely ignore the other.


4.3.8 Embarkation and Ticket Class
A deeper investigation reveals why passengers from Cherbourg (C) had a higher survival rate: a much higher proportion of first-class passengers boarded at Cherbourg compared to Queenstown or Southampton. The survival signal from Embarked is largely already captured by Pclass.
This is an example of a variable that appears predictive but is actually a proxy for another variable. Including such variables in a model can be misleading.

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
5.1 What is it?
Supervised learning means training a model on data where we already know the correct answers (the labels), so the model learns to predict those labels for new, unseen data.
Classification is a type of supervised learning where the output is a category (e.g. survived / not survived, spam / not spam, cat / dog).

Binary classification: two possible outcomes (like Titanic survived yes/no)
Multi-class classification: three or more outcomes (like Iris species: setosa / versicolor / virginica)

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
5.2 Common Classification Algorithms







Algorithm
Key Characteristic




Decision Trees
Simple, interpretable, easy to visualise


Logistic Regression
Probabilistic, good baseline model


Support Vector Machines
Strong with high-dimensional data


Naive Bayes
Fast, works well with text


Random Forests
Ensemble of trees, very powerful


Gradient Boosted Trees
Often best on tabular data


Deep Learning
Best for images, text, audio

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
6.1 The Core Workflow
library(rpart)
data(iris)

# 1. Set a random seed for reproducibility
set.seed(1987)

# 2. Split data into training (90%) and test (10%)
nobs <- nrow(iris)
train <- sample(nobs, 0.9 * nobs)
test <- setdiff(seq_len(nobs), train)

# 3. Train the model on training data ONLY
rpart_model <- rpart(Species ~ ., data = iris[train, ], method = "class")

# 4. Get predictions on the test set
pr <- predict(rpart_model, newdata = iris[test, ], type = "class")

# 5. Evaluate performance
tab <- table(pr, iris[test, ]$Species, dnn = c("predicted", "actual"))
print(tab)

accuracy <- 100 * sum(diag(tab)) / sum(tab)
print(paste('Model accuracy on test data =', round(accuracy, 2)))

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
section: 6.2 Why Split Into Train and Test?
text:
6.2 Why Split Into Train and Test?
A model will always look better on data it was trained on, because it has already “seen” that data. Evaluating on training data is like giving someone an exam with the answer sheet in front of them — it tells you nothing about how well they actually understand the material.
The test set must be:

Created before building the model
Completely separate from the training set (no overlap)
Representative of data the model will see in the real world


Important: The evaluation metrics you report should come from the test set, not the training set.

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
6.3 Reproducible Data Science
set.seed(1987)  # Always set this before any random operations
Setting a random seed ensures that every time the code is run (on any machine), it produces the exact same train/test split. This is essential for reproducible data science — other people must be able to reproduce your results exactly, or your findings cannot be trusted.

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
6.4 What is Overfitting / Overtraining?
Overfitting happens when a model learns the training data too well — including its noise and quirks — and fails to generalise to new data.
Example: If the model memorises that the 2 passengers with a blank Embarked value both survived, it will predict survival for any future record with a blank embarkation. This is not a real pattern, just noise — and the model will perform poorly on new data.

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
6.5 Model Drift and Data Drift

Data drift: The distribution of real-world data changes over time, so the model (trained on older data) becomes less accurate.
Model drift: The relationship between the input variables and the target variable changes over time.

Both of these mean that machine learning models need to be monitored and periodically retrained.

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
7.1 What is a Decision Tree?
A decision tree is one of the simplest and most intuitive machine learning algorithms. It works by splitting the data into groups based on a series of yes/no questions (conditions).
Analogy: Imagine a game of 20 questions. Each question splits the possibilities in two, and you keep narrowing down until you reach a final answer.

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
7.2 How it Works

Start with all the training data at the root node
Find the best attribute (column) and the best split point that separates the data into more “pure” groups
Create two branches (left = condition is true, right = condition is false)
Repeat for each sub-group (node) until:

All records in a node belong to the same class (it becomes a leaf node), or
There are no more useful attributes to split on

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
section: 7.3 How to Choose the Best Split?
text:
7.3 How to Choose the Best Split?
The algorithm uses a measure of impurity to decide which split is best. The goal is always to reduce impurity — i.e. to create groups that are as “pure” (dominated by a single class) as possible.
Two common impurity measures:
\[\text{Entropy}(t) = -\sum_{i=0}^{c-1} p(i|t) \log_2 p(i|t)\]
\[\text{Gini}(t) = 1 - \sum_{i=0}^{c-1} [p(i|t)]^2\]
Where c is the number of classes and p(i|t) is the probability that a record at node t belongs to class i.
Information Gain is calculated by comparing the impurity of the parent node to the weighted average impurity of the child nodes after the split. The split with the highest information gain is chosen.
Intuition: A node where 50% of records are class 0 and 50% are class 1 is maximally impure (entropy = 1). A node where 100% of records are the same class is perfectly pure (entropy = 0).

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
7.4 Advantages and Disadvantages







Advantages
Disadvantages




Easy to understand and visualise
Other algorithms usually outperform it


Can deal with non-linear data
Tends to overfit (especially deep trees)


No need to normalise data
Sensitive to small changes in data


Can be expressed as if/then rules
Does not handle missing data well

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
7.5 Handling Numeric Data
Since every node is a yes/no condition, numeric variables are handled by splitting on a threshold value (e.g. Age >= 13, Fare >= 23). The algorithm finds the best threshold automatically.

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
7.6 Pruning
Decision trees can grow very large and complex, overfitting the training data. Pruning means cutting off some of the lower branches to simplify the tree and improve generalisation.

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
7.7 Example — Titanic Decision Tree
The decision tree built on the Titanic dataset finds the following key splits (starting from the root):

Sex = male? → Most important split. Males generally did not survive.
For males: Age >= 13? → Younger boys had better survival odds
For females: Pclass >= 3? → 3rd class females had lower survival rates
Further splits on Fare and SibSp

This mirrors exactly what we expected from the exploratory analysis: gender is the most important predictor, followed by ticket class and age.

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
8.1 What is a Confusion Matrix?
After building a model, we compare its predictions against the actual values on the test set. The results are displayed in a confusion matrix.
For a binary classification problem (survived = 1, not survived = 0):




Actual: 0
Actual: 1




Predicted: 0
True Negative (TN)
False Negative (FN)


Predicted: 1
False Positive (FP)
True Positive (TP)

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
8.2 Titanic Example
          Actual
Predicted  0   1
        0  50  19   ← Predicted "not survived"
        1   4  17   ← Predicted "survived"

TN = 50: Correctly predicted “did not survive”
FN = 19: Person survived, but model predicted they didn’t (missed survivors)
FP = 4: Person didn’t survive, but model predicted they did
TP = 17: Correctly predicted “survived”

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
8.3 Accuracy
\[\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{17 + 50}{90} \approx 74\%\]

Note to self: Accuracy is not always the best metric — it can be misleading when classes are imbalanced. For example, if only 5% of passengers survived, a model that always predicts “not survived” would be 95% accurate but completely useless. More metrics (precision, recall, F1) will be covered in future classes.

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 2
text: Week 1 covered the foundations: the differences between data, information, and knowledge; definitions of AI, Machine Learning, and Deep Learning; the CRISP-DM and SEMMA workflows; types of ML (supervised, unsupervised, reinforcement learning); and a worked linear regression example.

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 2
text: Week 1 covered the foundations: the differences between data, information, and knowledge; definitions of AI, Machine Learning, and Deep Learning; the CRISP-DM and SEMMA workflows; types of ML (supervised, unsupervised, reinforcement learning); and a worked linear regression example.

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 2
section: 2 CRISP-DM and the Importance of Business Understanding
text:
2 CRISP-DM and the Importance of Business Understanding
Before diving into statistics and code, the lecture emphasised a critical lesson: the data understanding and data preparation stages of CRISP-DM are often more important than the modelling stage itself. The CRISP-DM cycle has six phases — Business Understanding, Data Understanding, Data Preparation, Modelling, Evaluation, and Deployment — and notice that two of those six are entirely about working with the data before any model is built.

2.1 The PayPal Churn Case Study
This real-world example illustrated how business understanding can matter more than fancy algorithms. PayPal was losing around 1 million merchants per year. After 17 years of trying various approaches, the problem was finally tackled not with advanced ML but with careful problem definition using simple tools (spreadsheets and SQL).
Key steps in their approach:

Exclude accounts that already cancelled: By the time someone cancels, it is too late — they have already migrated to a competitor like Stripe. Nothing can be done to win them back.
Exclude “one-and-dones”: Merchants who signed up briefly, had little revenue, and disappeared. Not worth chasing because the revenue was negligible.
Separate activation from retention: New merchants who had problems getting started (failed payments, onboarding issues) are a different problem for a different team. This is not churn — it is an onboarding issue.
Exclude false positives: Seasonal businesses (Halloween costume stores), subscription processors who only transact once a month, and merchants who were kicked off for violating terms.
Focus on revenue churn, not account churn: 90% of revenue came from 10% of merchants. Losing a merchant doing €100,000/month matters far more than losing one doing €500/month.

After all these exclusions, they looked at individual cases for their highest-value remaining merchants, identified 20 “killer scenarios” causing churn, and fixed them one by one — often just by having customer service call the merchant.
The takeaway: Always ask “What am I actually trying to solve?” before reaching for a machine learning model. Sometimes the best solution is careful problem decomposition combined with domain expertise.

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 2
section: 3 Basic Statistics: Means, Medians, Quartiles, and IQR
text:
3 Basic Statistics: Means, Medians, Quartiles, and IQR

3.1 Mean vs Median

Mean: The arithmetic average — sum all values and divide by the count. Sensitive to extreme values (outliers).
Median: The middle value when data is sorted from smallest to largest. Robust to outliers.

Why the difference matters: Imagine 11 people in a room, each earning around €100,000. The mean salary is roughly €100,000 and the median is also about €100,000 — they agree. Now Bill Gates and Jeff Bezos walk in. The mean salary jumps to perhaps €30 million, but the median barely changes — it just moves from the 6th to the 7th person’s salary, still around €100,000.
The mean no longer represents anyone in the group. The median still tells you what the “typical” person earns. This is why the media report the median age of COVID deaths rather than the average — it gives a more meaningful picture of who is affected.
Rule of thumb: When data is skewed or contains outliers, the median is usually more informative than the mean.


3.2 Quartiles and Interquartile Range (IQR)
To calculate quartiles:

Order the data from smallest to largest.
Split it into four equal groups.
Each group is a “quartile.”


Q1 (25th percentile): The boundary between the lowest 25% and the rest.
Q2 (50th percentile): This is the median.
Q3 (75th percentile): The boundary between the top 25% and the rest.
IQR = Q3 − Q1: The range covering the middle 50% of the data.

The IQR is useful because it tells you how spread out the “core” of your data is, ignoring extremes.

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 2
text:
4 Visualising Distributions: Univariate Plots
Univariate plots examine a single variable at a time. The three main types covered are histograms, density plots, and box plots. They all show the distribution (the spread and shape) of your data, but in different ways.

4.1 Histograms
A histogram divides the data into “bins” (ranges) and counts how many values fall into each bin. It shows frequency on the y-axis and the variable’s values on the x-axis. Think of it as a bar chart of your data’s distribution.

qplot(data, geom = "histogram", bins = 100)



4.2 Density Plots
A density plot is a smoothed version of a histogram. Instead of bars, you get a continuous curve. The y-axis shows density (not raw count), and the area under the curve sums to 1. It is useful for seeing the overall shape of the distribution without being distracted by bin choices.

qplot(data, geom = "density")



4.3 Box Plots
A box plot (or box-and-whisker plot) packs a lot of information into a compact display:

The box spans from Q1 to Q3 (the IQR — the middle 50% of data).
The line inside the box is the median.
The whiskers extend to the most extreme data points that are within 1.5 × IQR from the edges of the box.
Points beyond the whiskers are considered outliers.

Box plots are extremely useful for statisticians and data scientists, but as the lecturer noted, they are often not well understood by non-technical business users. So use them for your own analysis, but consider simpler visuals when presenting to stakeholders.

qplot(data, geom = "boxplot") + coord_flip()



4.4 The drawPlots Helper Function
The lecture introduced a handy helper function that draws all three plots side by side, with the mean shown in blue and the median in green:

drawPlots <- function(data, title) {
  p01 <- qplot(data, geom = "histogram", bins = 100) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = mean(data), size = 2, color = "blue") +
    geom_vline(xintercept = median(data), size = 2, color = "green")
  p02 <- qplot(data, geom = "density") +
    geom_vline(xintercept = mean(data), size = 2, color = "blue") +
    geom_vline(xintercept = median(data), size = 2, color = "green")
  p03 <- qplot(data, geom = "boxplot") +
    coord_flip() +
    geom_point(aes(x = mean(data), y = 0), size = 3, color = "blue")
  gridExtra::grid.arrange(p01, p02, p03, nrow = 1, top = title)
}

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 2
section: 5 Skewness: What It Is and Why It Matters
text:
5 Skewness: What It Is and Why It Matters

5.1 What Is Skewness?
Skewness describes how asymmetric a distribution is compared to a normal (bell-shaped) distribution.

Normal distribution: Symmetric. The mean and median are approximately equal. The histogram/density plot looks like a bell curve, centred around the middle.
Right (positive) skew: The “tail” of the distribution stretches to the right. Most values are clustered on the left, but a few very large values pull the mean to the right. Mean > Median.
Left (negative) skew: The tail stretches to the left. Most values cluster on the right, but a few very small values pull the mean left. Mean < Median.


# Normal distribution — mean ≈ median
data <- rnorm(10000, mean = 0, sd = 1)
drawPlots(data, "Normal distribution (median ~= mean)")

# Left (negative) skew — median > mean
data <- rbeta(10000, 5, 1)
drawPlots(data, "Left skewed (median-green > mean-blue)")

# Right (positive) skew — median < mean
data <- rbeta(10000, 1, 5)
drawPlots(data, "Right skewed (median-green < mean-blue)")



5.2 Real-World Examples of Skewed Data
A lot of real-life data is skewed:

Income: Most people earn moderate amounts, but a few earn enormously high salaries (right skew).
Average spend per customer: Most customers spend a little, a few spend a lot.
Distance travelled to work: Most people live reasonably close; some commute very long distances.
Animal body weights: Most mammals are small; a few (elephants, whales) are extremely large.



5.3 Why Skewness Matters for Machine Learning
The lecture demonstrated this using the mammals dataset (from the openintro package), which contains body weight and brain weight for various mammals.
The problem: Both body weight and brain weight are extremely right-skewed — most mammals are small, but a few (like elephants) are huge. When you plot the raw data, almost all points cluster near zero, and you can barely see any relationship.
Step 1 — Build a model on raw data:

model1 <- lm(body_wt ~ brain_wt, data = mammals)
r_sq1 <- cor(model1$fitted.values, mammals$body_wt)^2
# R² ≈ 0.87

Step 2 — Apply a log transformation:
Taking the logarithm of skewed data compresses the large values and stretches the small ones, making the distribution more symmetrical (closer to normal).

mammals$log_body_wt <- log(mammals$body_wt)
mammals$log_brain_wt <- log(mammals$brain_wt)

When you plot the log-transformed data, you can now clearly see a linear relationship between log(brain weight) and log(body weight).
Step 3 — Build a model on transformed data:

model2 <- lm(log_body_wt ~ log_brain_wt, data = mammals)
r_sq2 <- cor(model2$fitted.values, mammals$log_body_wt)^2
# R² ≈ 0.92

The R² improved from 0.87 to 0.92. As the slides noted, this represents a 37.79% reduction in error rate. That is a significant improvement gained purely from transforming the data — not from changing the algorithm.
Key insight: If your data is skewed, consider applying a log transformation (or other transformations) before modelling. This is a form of feature engineering and can dramatically improve model performance.

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 2
text:
6 Packages and the qplot Function

6.1 Required Packages

library(ggplot2)     # Plotting — qplot and ggplot functions
library(gridExtra)   # Arrange multiple plots in a grid
library(datasets)    # Built-in R datasets (iris, etc.)
library(corrplot)    # Correlation plot visualisations
library(titanic)     # Titanic dataset
library(psych)       # pairs.panels() for pairs plots
library(openintro)   # mammals dataset and others

If you get errors like could not find function "xxxxx", it almost certainly means you need to install and/or load the package first.


6.2 The qplot Function (Quick Plot)
qplot is part of ggplot2 and provides a quick way to create plots:

One variable → defaults to a histogram.
Two variables → defaults to a scatterplot.
Change the plot type with the geom parameter (e.g., geom = "density", geom = "boxplot").
Use main for title, xlab for x-axis label, ylab for y-axis label.

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 2
text:
7 Datasets in R
R comes with many built-in datasets through the datasets package. Some notable ones:

iris: 150 observations of iris flowers (3 species, 4 measurements each). A classic ML dataset.
Titanic: Survival data for passengers.
airquality: New York air quality measurements.
anscombe: Four datasets with nearly identical summary statistics but very different distributions — a great demonstration of why you should always visualise your data.


library(help = "datasets")  # See all available datasets
data(iris)                    # Load a specific dataset

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 2
text:
8 Data Exploration: The Iris Dataset
Data exploration is critical to the success of any ML project. As the lecturer emphasised: it is more important than anything you do in the model-building stage, including picking an algorithm. You should look at missing data, check for incomplete data, perform statistical analysis, and create visualisations. The insights you gain here should actively guide your feature engineering and modelling decisions — not just be a box-ticking exercise.

8.1 About the Iris Dataset
Introduced by Ronald Fisher in 1936, the Iris dataset contains 150 observations: 50 each of three species (setosa, versicolor, virginica). Four numeric features are measured: Sepal.Length, Sepal.Width, Petal.Length, and Petal.Width. The goal is to classify species based on these measurements.


8.2 Initial Exploration

data(iris)
dim(iris)       # 150 rows, 5 columns
names(iris)     # "Sepal.Length" "Sepal.Width" "Petal.Length" "Petal.Width" "Species"
str(iris)       # Shows types: 4 numeric columns + 1 factor (Species)
summary(iris[, -5])  # Summary stats for numeric columns only



8.3 Column Types
Getting column types right is crucial in R:

numeric / integer: Numbers.
character: Text strings.
logical: TRUE/FALSE.
factor: Categorical variables with a fixed set of levels (like Species with 3 levels).

You can convert between types with as.numeric(), as.character(), as.factor(), etc. But not all conversions are possible — as.numeric("three") produces NA with a warning.


8.4 Histograms — Iris

p01 <- qplot(iris$Sepal.Length, geom = "histogram", bins = 10,
             main = "Histogram on Sepal.Length", xlab = "Sepal.Length")
p02 <- qplot(iris$Sepal.Width, geom = "histogram", bins = 10,
             main = "Histogram on Sepal.Width", xlab = "Sepal.Width")
p03 <- qplot(iris$Petal.Length, geom = "histogram", bins = 10,
             main = "Histogram on Petal.Length", xlab = "Petal.Length")
p04 <- qplot(iris$Petal.Width, geom = "histogram", bins = 10,
             main = "Histogram on Petal.Width", xlab = "Petal.Width")
gridExtra::grid.arrange(p01, p02, p03, p04)

What to notice: Petal.Length and Petal.Width show a clear bimodal distribution (two humps). This suggests there might be distinct groups in the data — which makes sense because setosa has much smaller petals than the other two species.


8.5 Density Plots — Iris

p01 <- qplot(iris$Sepal.Length, geom = "density",
             main = "Density plot on Sepal.Length", xlab = "Sepal.Length")
# ... similar for the other three variables
gridExtra::grid.arrange(p01, p02, p03, p04)

The density plots confirm the same patterns seen in the histograms, but the smooth curves make the bimodal shape of the petal measurements even more obvious.


8.6 Box Plots — Iris (by Species)

p01 <- qplot(Species, Sepal.Length, data = iris, geom = "boxplot",
             main = "Boxplot on Sepal.Length")
# ... similar for other variables
gridExtra::grid.arrange(p01, p02, p03, p04)

What to notice: When we split by species, the box plots reveal that setosa is very clearly separated from the other two species on Petal.Length and Petal.Width (almost no overlap). Versicolor and virginica overlap more, but are still distinguishable. Sepal.Width is an interesting variable — setosa actually has wider sepals than the other species.


8.7 Scatter Plots — Iris (Multivariate)
Scatter plots show the relationship between two variables at once. By colouring by species, we can see how well the species separate:

p01 <- qplot(Sepal.Width, Sepal.Length, data = iris, colour = Species,
             main = "Sepal.Width v Sepal.Length")
p02 <- qplot(Petal.Length, Petal.Width, data = iris, colour = Species,
             main = "Petal.Length v Petal.Width")
# ... more combinations
gridExtra::grid.arrange(p01, p02, p03, p04)

Key insight: The Petal.Length vs Petal.Width scatter plot shows the clearest separation between all three species. This tells you these are likely the most useful features for classification.


8.8 Correlation Analysis
Correlation measures the linear relationship between two numeric variables, ranging from −1 (perfect negative) to +1 (perfect positive), with 0 meaning no linear relationship.

corr_mat <- cor(iris[, -5])   # Exclude Species (not numeric)
corrplot.mixed(corr_mat)

Key correlations in iris:

Petal.Length and Petal.Width: 0.96 (very strong positive — as petals get longer, they also get wider).
Sepal.Length and Petal.Length: 0.87 (strong positive).
Sepal.Length and Petal.Width: 0.82 (strong positive).
Sepal.Width and Petal.Length: −0.43 (moderate negative).
Sepal.Length and Sepal.Width: −0.12 (very weak negative — almost no relationship).



8.9 Pairs Plots
A pairs plot combines histograms (on the diagonal), scatter plots (below the diagonal), and correlation values (above the diagonal) into a single comprehensive view:

pairs.panels(iris[, -5], method = "pearson",
             hist.col = "#00AFBB", density = TRUE, ellipses = FALSE)

This is one of the most useful exploratory tools because it gives you a full picture of all pairwise relationships at a glance.

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 2
section: 9 The Titanic Dataset: A Brief Introduction
text:
9 The Titanic Dataset: A Brief Introduction
The Titanic dataset is a classic ML classification problem: Can we predict which passengers survived the shipwreck?

9.1 Loading and Inspecting

data(titanic_train)
str(titanic_train)   # 891 obs. of 12 variables



9.2 Understanding the Variables








Variable
Meaning
Notes




PassengerId
Unique identifier
Drop for modelling (unique per row)


Survived
Did they survive? (0 = No, 1 = Yes)
This is our target variable


Pclass
Ticket class (1st, 2nd, 3rd)
Important — consider converting to factor


Name
Passenger name
Drop (not predictive by itself)


Sex
Male or Female
Very important — women more likely survived


Age
Age in years
Children more likely survived; has 177 NAs


SibSp
Number of siblings/spouses aboard
Needs investigation


Parch
Number of parents/children aboard
Needs investigation


Ticket
Ticket number
Probably drop, unless embedded patterns


Fare
Ticket price
May relate to survival (wealth/class proxy)


Cabin
Cabin number
687 blanks — might indicate location on ship


Embarked
Port of embarkation (C/Q/S)
Test whether it affects survival





9.3 Exploring Unique Values and Missing Data

# Count unique values per column
uniq_len <- function(x) { length(unique(x)) }
sapply(titanic_train, uniq_len)

# Count missing/blank values per column
countBlank <- function(x) { sum(is.na(x) | x == "") }
sapply(titanic_train, countBlank)

Key findings:

PassengerId and Name have 891 unique values (same as row count) — unique identifiers, drop both.
Age has 177 missing values — need to decide whether to fill them in (impute) or drop the column.
Cabin has 687 blank values (77% missing!) — possibly useful if you extract deck letter, but the missingness itself is a challenge.
Embarked has 2 blank values — easy to fill.

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 2
text:
10 Feature Engineering
Feature engineering is the process of transforming raw data into features that are more useful for ML models. It happens after data exploration and is more important than your choice of algorithm. Common tasks include:

Removing irrelevant columns (e.g., PassengerId, Name).
Converting column types (e.g., Pclass from integer to factor, since it is categorical).
Handling missing values — filling them in (imputation) or dropping affected rows/columns.
Transforming or splitting columns (e.g., extracting a title like “Mr” or “Mrs” from the Name column; creating an “is child” flag from Age).
Handling skewed variables — applying log or other transformations.
Scaling numeric variables — ensuring all variables are on similar scales so no single variable dominates due to its units.

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 2
text:
11 Summary of Key Takeaways

Business understanding comes first. The PayPal churn example shows that problem decomposition and domain knowledge can be more powerful than any algorithm.
Data exploration is not optional. It should actively drive your feature engineering decisions, not just be a formality before modelling.
Understand mean vs median. Use median for skewed data; the mean can be misleading when outliers are present.
Know your univariate plots. Histograms, density plots, and box plots each show distribution — learn to read all three.
Skewness matters for ML. Log transformations on skewed data can substantially improve model performance (the mammals example improved R² from 0.87 to 0.92).
Explore relationships between variables using scatter plots, correlation matrices, and pairs plots.
Feature engineering is more important than algorithm choice. Cleaning, transforming, and preparing your data well will have a bigger impact on results than picking the “best” algorithm.
